Land Rover Range Rover Velar P300 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover Velar P300 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Range Rover Velar P300 engine (204PT) typically costs £4,000–£6,500 supply only. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800. These are specialist-only replacements — the Ingenium 2.0L petrol requires Land Rover specialist expertise. A water pump replacement costs £400–£800.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Range Rover Velar P300 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the 204PT petrol Ingenium is significantly more reliable than the diesel variant. The main concerns are water pump failure on pre-2020 models (£400–£800) and timing chain stretch on higher-mileage examples if oil changes are neglected (£1,500–£3,000). Regular oil changes (12,000 miles max) with the correct 0W-20 C5 spec are essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Range Rover Velar P300?

acceptedAnswer.text: Water pump failure (coolant loss, whining noise, £400–£800), timing chain stretch on higher-mileage or poorly maintained examples (£1,500–£3,000), and cooling system leaks (hoses, thermostat housing, £300–£1,000). Direct injector coking also occurs — walnut blasting every 60,000 miles is recommended.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover Velar P300 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Range Rover Velar P300 values range from £18,000–£50,000 depending on year, condition and mileage. A £4,000–£6,500 reconditioned engine represents 10–35% of vehicle value — well within our 60% Land Rover scrap threshold. For most examples, replacement is financially viable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Range Rover Velar P300 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The P300 uses the 204PT engine code — JLR's 2.0-litre Ingenium four-cylinder petrol. This is the same engine as the P250 but with higher output tuning. The code is stamped on the engine block; your VIN and vehicle documentation will also confirm the specification.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the water pump issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — water pump replacement (£400–£800) is a straightforward repair on the 204PT. The pre-2020 water pumps have a plastic impeller that can fail; the revised unit is more durable. At the first sign of whining noise from the front of the engine or coolant loss, book an inspection immediately — ignored water pump failure can lead to overheating and serious engine damage.

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Velar P300 engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £80–£120/hr (independent Land Rover specialist) or £140–£190/hr (London/SE dealer).

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover Velar P300 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Worth it on a vehicle worth £25,000–£50,000
Replace with revised parts for a permanent fix
Replacement often £4,000–£6,500 — assess vehicle value carefully

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Range Rover Velar P300? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2020 model (revised water pump and cooling system)
* Full Land Rover service history with correct 0W-20 C5 oil specification
* Recent timing chain inspection (for higher-mileage examples)
avoidIf: * Any evidence of coolant loss or overheating (water pump or cooling system failure)
* Poor service history — the 204PT is less forgiving of neglect than the old V6s
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — these engines can hide expensive problems
* Pre-2020 model with no water pump history and signs of coolant loss

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The P300 is one of the most refined petrol engines in the Velar range — smooth, responsive and genuinely enjoyable. The 204PT is significantly more reliable than the diesel Ingenium, with water pump and cooling system issues being the main concerns. Post-2020 examples with the revised water pump are the sweet spot. Buy on history, maintain the cooling system, and the P300 will serve you well.

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover Velar P300 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Velar P300 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup (timing chain stretch warning)
* Service History — 12,000 mile oil changes are essential; 8,000–10,000 is safer
* Coolant Level — Check for any loss — water pump and cooling system leaks are common
* Water Pump Health — Any whining noise from the front of the engine? (pump failure warning)
* Engine Performance — Test acceleration; any hesitation or flat spots?
* Direct Injection Carbon — Has the intake had a walnut blast service? (every 60k miles recommended)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Cooling system and O2 sensor codes are common
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst/sensor issues)
